questions.question: What does the acronym ASIC stand for in the context of integrated circuit design?
options: Application-Specific Integrated Circuit
Advanced System Interface Controller
Automated Sequential Instruction Code
Analog Signal Input Converter
Asynchronous Serial Interconnection Channel

questions.question: In a standard CMOS digital logic gate (e.g., an inverter), what is the primary role of a P-type Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor (PMOS) device?
options: To act as a pull-down network, connecting the output to ground when active.
To act as a pull-up network, connecting the output to VDD when active.
To store charge and provide memory functionality.
To amplify the input signal voltage.
To convert an analog signal into a digital one.

questions.question: In the context of multi-processor systems and shared memory, what defines "Sequential Consistency" as proposed by Lamport?
options: The result of any execution is the same as if the operations of all processors were executed in some sequential order, and the operations of each individual processor appear in this sequence in the order specified by its program.
Writes from one processor are immediately visible to all other processors.
Memory operations are only ordered if they are explicitly synchronized by software.
Each processor observes its own memory operations in program order, but not necessarily those of other processors.
Data is only consistent within a single cache line, not across the entire memory system.

questions.question: In digital IC design, which power reduction technique involves temporarily shutting off the power supply to an idle functional block or an entire region of a chip to eliminate both dynamic and static power consumption in that block?
options: Clock Gating
Voltage Scaling
Frequency Scaling
Power Gating
Body Biasing

questions.question: In computer architecture, what does the term "Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)" primarily define?
options: The physical layout and interconnects of transistors on a microchip.
The maximum operating frequency and power consumption limits of a processor.
The abstract interface between the software (programmer/compiler) and the hardware (processor).
The specific algorithms used for cache coherence protocols.
The type of memory technology used (e.g., SRAM, DRAM).
